_doc: One SUBTLE verifiably-FALSE sibling per canonical theorem. Unlike false_siblings_50.json (blatant), each item here is a single meaning-changing edit that stays plausible to a careful reader: off-by-one boundaries, dropped hypotheses, false converses, strict/nonstrict flips, quantifier-scope shifts, widened domains. Every item is unambiguously FALSE with a concrete counterexample. Numeric counterexamples are machine-checked by verify_false_siblings.py against the same supported check expressions.

siblings.false_stmt: Every prime number is strictly greater than 2
siblings.counterexample: 2 is prime and 2 is not strictly greater than 2

siblings.false_stmt: Every prime number is at least 3
siblings.counterexample: 2 is prime and 2 < 3

siblings.false_stmt: Since 1 is not prime, 1 is composite
siblings.counterexample: 1 is neither prime nor composite: a composite number has a factorization into smaller factors greater than 1, which 1 lacks, so calling 1 composite is false

siblings.false_stmt: 1 is a prime number
siblings.counterexample: 1 has only one positive divisor (itself), so 1 is not prime

siblings.false_stmt: For natural numbers, if m divides n then m equals n
siblings.counterexample: 2 divides 4 but 2 is not equal to 4

siblings.false_stmt: Every natural number divides its predecessor
siblings.counterexample: 3 does not divide 2

siblings.false_stmt: Zero divides every natural number
siblings.counterexample: 0 does not divide 1 (1 is not a multiple of 0)

siblings.false_stmt: A natural number divides zero if and only if that number is zero
siblings.counterexample: 3 divides 0, yet 3 is not zero, so the 'only if' direction fails

siblings.false_stmt: If a number divides the sum of two numbers, then it divides each of them
siblings.counterexample: 2 divides 1+3=4 but 2 does not divide 1

siblings.false_stmt: If a number divides a product of two numbers, it divides at least one of the factors
siblings.counterexample: 6 divides 4*9=36 but 6 divides neither 4 nor 9

siblings.false_stmt: For integers a and b, a mod b - b * (a div b) = a
Any natural number modulo its successor is zero
siblings.counterexample: 2 mod 3 = 2, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: Any natural number modulo zero is zero
siblings.counterexample: in Lean, 5 mod 0 = 5, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: The square of any real number is strictly positive
siblings.counterexample: 0 squared is 0, which is not strictly positive

siblings.false_stmt: In any additive monoid, including the natural-number-indexed string concatenation monoid, addition is commutative
siblings.counterexample: string concatenation is an additive-style monoid operation but 'ab' ++ 'c' = 'abc' while 'c' ++ 'ab' = 'cab', so it is not commutative

siblings.false_stmt: In any monoid, multiplication is commutative
siblings.counterexample: 2x2 matrices form a monoid; [[1,1],[0,1]] times [[1,0],[1,1]] differs from the reversed product, so multiplication is not commutative

siblings.false_stmt: In any additive group, subtraction is associative
siblings.counterexample: in the integers, (1-1)-1 = -1 but 1-(1-1) = 1

siblings.false_stmt: In any algebraic structure with a binary product, multiplication is associative
siblings.counterexample: the cross product on 3D vectors is a binary product but i x (i x j) = -j while (i x i) x j = 0, so it is not associative

siblings.false_stmt: Adding zero to any element returns zero
siblings.counterexample: in the integers, 5 + 0 = 5, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: There is an element that, added on the left to any element, returns that element; and that element is 1
siblings.counterexample: the left identity for addition is 0, not 1; 1 + 5 = 6, not 5

siblings.false_stmt: Any element multiplied by one is one
siblings.counterexample: in the integers, 5 * 1 = 5, not 1

siblings.false_stmt: One added to any element is that element
siblings.counterexample: in the integers, 1 + 5 = 6, not 5

siblings.false_stmt: The absolute value of any real number equals its square
siblings.counterexample: for x=2, |2| = 2 but 2^2 = 4, so they are not equal

siblings.false_stmt: The absolute value of any element is strictly positive
siblings.counterexample: |0| = 0, which is not strictly positive

siblings.false_stmt: The degree of a sum of two polynomials is strictly less than the maximum of their degrees
siblings.counterexample: for p = X and q = X^2, deg(p+q) = 2 which equals (not is less than) max(deg p, deg q) = 2

siblings.false_stmt: Over any commutative semiring, the degree of a product of two polynomials equals the sum of their degrees
siblings.counterexample: over Z/4Z, (2X)(2X) = 4X^2 = 0 has degree -infinity, not deg(2X)+deg(2X)=2; the no-zero-divisors hypothesis is needed

siblings.false_stmt: Every set is a proper subset of itself
siblings.counterexample: a proper subset must be strictly smaller, but no set is strictly smaller than itself

siblings.false_stmt: For sets s, t, u, if s is a subset of t and s is a subset of u, then t is a subset of u
siblings.counterexample: s = {}, t = {1}, u = {2}: s subset t and s subset u, but {1} is not a subset of {2}

siblings.false_stmt: Set difference is commutative
For all sets s and t, the union of s and t equals the union of t and s only when s and t are disjoint
siblings.counterexample: union is commutative for ALL s,t; e.g. s={1,2}, t={2,3} are not disjoint yet s union t = t union s = {1,2,3}, so the disjointness restriction is false

siblings.false_stmt: Set difference is associative
Set difference is associative
There is a nonempty set that is a subset of every set
siblings.counterexample: any nonempty set contains some element x, but the empty set does not contain x, so no nonempty set is a subset of every set

siblings.false_stmt: The union of any set with the empty set is empty
siblings.counterexample: {1} union {} = {1}, not the empty set

siblings.false_stmt: The intersection of any set with the empty set is that set
siblings.counterexample: {1} intersect {} = {}, not {1}

siblings.false_stmt: The cardinality of the empty finite set is the cardinality of a singleton minus its element, but counted from one, so it is 1
siblings.counterexample: the empty finite set has 0 elements, so its cardinality is 0, not 1

siblings.false_stmt: A finite set with exactly one element has cardinality 0
siblings.counterexample: the singleton {a} contains the element a, so its cardinality is 1, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: The factorial of any natural number is strictly greater than that number
siblings.counterexample: 2! = 2, which is not strictly greater than 2

siblings.false_stmt: For all natural numbers n and k, C(n, n-k) = C(n, k)
siblings.counterexample: n=2, k=3: with natural subtraction n-k = 0, so C(2,0)=1 but C(2,3)=0; the hypothesis k <= n was dropped

siblings.false_stmt: C(0, n) = 1 for all natural numbers n
C(n, n-1) = 1 for all natural numbers n
The real square root function is always strictly positive
siblings.counterexample: sqrt(0) = 0, which is not strictly positive

siblings.false_stmt: For every real number x, the square root of x squared equals x
siblings.counterexample: x=-2: sqrt((-2)^2) = sqrt(4) = 2, not -2; the hypothesis 0 <= x was dropped

siblings.false_stmt: For every real number x, sqrt(x) * sqrt(x) = x
siblings.counterexample: x=-1: sqrt(-1) = 0 in Lean's real sqrt, so the product is 0, not -1; the hypothesis 0 <= x was dropped

siblings.false_stmt: In a group, every element times its own square equals the identity
siblings.counterexample: this would force a^3 = 1 for every a; in the integers under addition (written additively, 3a = 0), take a = 1: 3*1 = 3, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: In a group, the inverse of an element times that same inverse equals the identity
siblings.counterexample: a-inverse times a-inverse equals the identity only when a-inverse is its own inverse; in the integers under addition take a = 1, so a-inverse = -1 and (-1) + (-1) = -2, not 0

siblings.false_stmt: In a group, the inverse of the inverse of an element equals its inverse
siblings.counterexample: in the integers under addition, the element 3 has inverse -3; the inverse of -3 is 3, not -3

siblings.false_stmt: In any monoid, if a*b = a*c then b = c
siblings.counterexample: the natural numbers under multiplication form a monoid; 0*1 = 0*2 = 0 but 1 is not 2; left-cancellation needs more than a monoid

siblings.false_stmt: Every element is strictly less than itself
siblings.counterexample: 0 is not strictly less than 0

siblings.false_stmt: For all elements a, b, c, if a <= b and a <= c then b <= c
